questions.question: The binary operation [] is defined as follows. P=T,Q=T gives P[]Q=T; P=T,Q=F gives P[]Q=T; P=F,Q=T gives P[]Q=F; P=F,Q=F gives P[]Q=T. Which one of the following is equivalent to P v Q? A. (not Q) [] (not P)  B. P [] (not Q)  C. (not P) [] Q  D. (not P) [] (not Q)
questions.to_find: Which expression using the operation [] is logically equivalent to P v Q

understand.plain: First read the truth table to find a formula for the new operation []. The output P[]Q is TRUE in three rows and FALSE in only one row, the row where P=F and Q=T. So P[]Q is false exactly when (not P) and Q are both true, meaning P[]Q = not((not P) and Q) = P or (not Q). Now plug each option's two inputs into this rule X[]Y = X or (not Y) and check which simplifies to P v Q. Option B uses X=P and Y=not Q, giving P or not(not Q) = P or Q, which is exactly what we want.

keywords.explain: An operation is fully described by its output in every row; note the single row that gives FALSE and negate that condition to get a clean formula.
keywords.example: P[]Q is FALSE only at P=F, Q=T, so P[]Q = not((not P) and Q).

keywords.explain: not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B), which lets you turn the 'false condition' into an OR formula.
keywords.example: not((not P) and Q) = P or (not Q).

keywords.explain: Once X[]Y = X or (not Y) is known, evaluate each option by substituting its own left and right inputs for X and Y.
keywords.example: For B, X=P and Y=(not Q), so X[]Y = P or not(not Q) = P or Q.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='498' height='228' class='bg'/><rect x='30' y='25' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><rect x='90' y='25' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><rect x='150' y='25' width='90' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='52' y='47' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='112' y='47' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='168' y='47' class='lbl ink'>P[]Q</text><text x='52' y='80' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='112' y='80' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='185' y='80' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='52' y='105' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='112' y='105' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='185' y='105' class='sub ink'>T</text><rect x='30' y='113' width='210' height='26' class='hi'/><text x='52' y='132' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='112' y='132' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='185' y='132' class='no'>F</text><text x='52' y='160' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='112' y='160' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='185' y='160' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='280' y='100' class='lbl ink'>False only at</text><text x='280' y='128' class='lbl ink'>P=F, Q=T</text><text x='280' y='170' class='lbl ok'>P[]Q = P or not Q</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The truth table for P[]Q with the single FALSE row (P=F, Q=T) highlighted, showing P[]Q equals P or not Q.

given.aim: Express the operation [] in closed form and then identify the option that equals P v Q.

terms.meaning: Two propositional variables, each true (T) or false (F)
terms.example: P=T, Q=F is the second row of the table
terms.connects: Inputs to the operation [] and to the target formula P v Q

terms.meaning: The defined operation; TRUE except in the single row P=F, Q=T
terms.example: P[]Q = P or (not Q) once the table is decoded
terms.connects: The building block used inside every answer option

terms.meaning: Ordinary disjunction; the target expression to be matched
terms.example: P v Q is false only when both P and Q are false
terms.connects: The formula each option is tested against

terms.meaning: The left and right operands plugged into X[]Y = X or (not Y) for each choice
terms.example: For B, X=P and Y=(not Q)
terms.connects: Substituting them tells whether the option equals P v Q

given.plan: Decode the table as P[]Q = P or (not Q), then for each option substitute its operands into X[]Y = X or (not Y) and simplify; the one equal to P v Q is the answer.

steps.title: Decode the operation from the table
steps.formula_raw: not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B)
steps.apply: P[]Q is FALSE in exactly one row, P=F and Q=T, i.e. when (not P) and Q are both true. So P[]Q = not((not P) and Q). By De Morgan's law this is (not(not P)) or (not Q) = P or (not Q). Hence the operation is X[]Y = X or (not Y).
steps.note: Negating the unique 'false' condition is the fastest way to read a truth table into a formula.

steps.title: Test option B
steps.formula_raw: P [] (not Q) = P or not(not Q) = P or Q
steps.apply: Substitute X=P and Y=(not Q) into X[]Y = X or (not Y): P[](not Q) = P or not(not Q) = P or Q. This is exactly P v Q, so option B is equivalent. (Checking the rest: A gives not Q or P = P or not Q; C gives not P or not Q; D gives not P or Q. None of these equals P v Q.)
steps.note: Double negation not(not Q) collapses back to Q, which is what produces the clean P or Q.

solution.result: P [] (not Q) = P or Q, so option B is equivalent to P v Q.

questions.formula_note: Decoding the table via De Morgan gives X[]Y = X or (not Y); feeding Y = not Q makes the double negation collapse to P or Q, so B is the answer.

questions.question: Which one of the following propositional logic formulas is TRUE when exactly two of p, q and r are TRUE? A. ((p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r)  B. (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r)  C. ((p -> q) and r) or (p and q and not r)  D. (not(p <-> q) and r) and (p and q and not r)
questions.answer: B. (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r)
questions.to_find: The formula that is TRUE in exactly the cases where exactly two of p, q, r are true

understand.plain: We want a formula true precisely in the three assignments that make exactly two of p, q, r true: {p,q} true with r false, {p,r} true with q false, and {q,r} true with p false. A neat way to capture the two cases that contain r is to say 'r is true and exactly one of p, q is true'. The phrase 'exactly one of p, q' is not(p <-> q), since the biconditional is false when p and q differ. Adding the separate case p and q true with r false covers the third assignment. Option B is exactly (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r).

keywords.explain: True when p and q are equal; therefore not(p <-> q) is true exactly when p and q differ (exactly one of them is true).
keywords.example: p=T, q=F gives p <-> q = F, so not(p <-> q) = T.

keywords.explain: Among p, q, r, 'exactly two true' means one variable is false and the other two are true, which has three possible assignments.
keywords.example: p=T, q=T, r=F is one such assignment.

keywords.explain: Group the three 'exactly two' assignments into those with r=T (then exactly one of p,q is true) and the single one with r=F (then p and q are both true).
keywords.example: r=T forces 'exactly one of p,q'; r=F forces 'both p and q'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='218' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Exactly two of p, q, r true:</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='150' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='78' class='sub ink'>p,q true ; r false</text><rect x='30' y='100' width='150' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='123' class='sub ink'>p,r true ; q false</text><rect x='30' y='145' width='150' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='168' class='sub ink'>q,r true ; p false</text><text x='210' y='78' class='sub mut'>r=F, both p,q</text><text x='210' y='123' class='sub mut'>r=T, one of p,q</text><text x='210' y='168' class='sub mut'>r=T, one of p,q</text><text x='20' y='205' class='lbl ok'>(not(p<->q) and r) or (p and q and not r)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The three 'exactly two true' assignments grouped into r=true cases (exactly one of p,q) and the r=false case (both p,q), matching option B.

given.aim: Find the formula that is true exactly on the three assignments where two of p, q, r are true.

terms.meaning: Three Boolean variables, each true or false
terms.example: p=T, q=T, r=F is an 'exactly two true' case
terms.connects: The variables whose count of TRUEs the formula must detect

terms.meaning: True exactly when p and q have different truth values (exactly one is true)
terms.example: p=T, q=F gives not(p <-> q) = T
terms.connects: Captures 'exactly one of p, q' inside the r=true branch

terms.term: p and q and not r
terms.meaning: True only when p and q are both true and r is false
terms.connects: Supplies the single 'exactly two true' case that does not contain r

terms.meaning: Joining the r-true branch and the r-false branch with OR covers all three target assignments
terms.example: (not(p<->q) and r) or (p and q and not r)
terms.connects: The full formula whose truth set must equal the 'exactly two true' set

given.plan: List the three 'exactly two true' assignments, encode the two r=true ones as 'r and exactly one of p,q' = (not(p<->q) and r) and the r=false one as (p and q and not r), then OR them and match an option.

steps.title: Encode 'r true and exactly one of p, q true'
steps.formula_raw: not(p <-> q) is true iff p and q differ
steps.apply: Two of the target assignments have r=T: {p,r} (q false) and {q,r} (p false). Both mean r is true and exactly one of p, q is true. 'Exactly one of p, q' is not(p <-> q), since the biconditional is true only when p, q agree. So these two cases are exactly (not(p <-> q) and r).
steps.note: Using the biconditional to mean 'p and q are equal' makes its negation mean 'they differ'.

steps.title: Add the r=false case and match the option
steps.formula_raw: (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r)
steps.apply: The remaining target assignment is p=T, q=T, r=F, written p and q and not r. Joining both branches with OR gives (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r), which is true on exactly the three desired rows and false everywhere else. This is option B. (Option A uses (p<->q) so it fires when p,q are equal, wrong; C uses (p->q); D uses AND between the branches, which can never be satisfied.)
steps.note: Each branch alone is too narrow; only their OR hits all three 'exactly two' rows without adding extras.

solution.result: (not(p <-> q) and r) or (p and q and not r) is true exactly when two of p, q, r are true, so option B is correct.

questions.formula_note: Reading not(p <-> q) as 'exactly one of p, q is true' lets the r=true cases collapse to (not(p<->q) and r); OR-ing the r=false case (p and q and not r) gives B.

questions.question: Which one of the following Boolean expressions is NOT a tautology? A. ((a -> b) and (b -> c)) -> (a -> c)  B. (a -> c) -> (not b -> (a and c))  C. (a and b and c) -> (c or a)  D. a -> (b -> a)
questions.to_find: The Boolean expression that fails to be true under every assignment (the one that is NOT a tautology)

understand.plain: A tautology is a formula that is true for every assignment of its variables. To show a formula is NOT a tautology we only need ONE assignment that makes it false. Options A, C, D are standard valid laws (hypothetical syllogism, conjunction implies a contained disjunction, and 'a implies anything implies a'), so each is a tautology. Option B fails: take a=F, b=F, c=F. Then (a -> c) is true, but (not b -> (a and c)) becomes (T -> F) which is false, so the whole implication is true -> false = false.

keywords.explain: A propositional formula that evaluates to TRUE under every possible truth assignment of its variables.
keywords.example: a or not a is a tautology; a and not a is not.

keywords.explain: To prove a formula is not a tautology, exhibit one assignment that makes it false; one row is enough.
keywords.example: a=F, b=F, c=F makes option B false.

keywords.term: Implication is false only as T -> F
keywords.explain: An implication X -> Y is false exactly when X is true and Y is false; this is where you look for a counterexample.
keywords.example: (a->c) true while (not b -> (a and c)) false makes B false.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='208' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Try a=F, b=F, c=F in option B</text><text x='30' y='75' class='lbl ink'>a -> c  =  F -> F  =  T</text><text x='30' y='110' class='lbl ink'>a and c  =  F</text><text x='30' y='140' class='lbl ink'>not b -> (a and c)  =  T -> F  =  F</text><rect x='20' y='160' width='430' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='184' class='lbl no'>B  =  T -> F  =  FALSE  (not a tautology)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Evaluation of option B at a=F, b=F, c=F showing the outer implication becomes true -> false = false, so B is not a tautology.

given.aim: Identify which of the four implications is falsifiable, i.e. not true under every assignment.

terms.meaning: Three Boolean variables ranging over true/false
terms.example: The falsifying row is a=F, b=F, c=F
terms.connects: The variables over which 'tautology' is checked

terms.meaning: ((a -> b) and (b -> c)) -> (a -> c): hypothetical syllogism, always true
terms.example: Chaining a->b and b->c gives a->c
terms.connects: A known tautology, so not the answer

terms.meaning: (a -> c) -> (not b -> (a and c)): the candidate that fails
terms.connects: The expression we must show is NOT a tautology

terms.meaning: (a and b and c) -> (c or a) and a -> (b -> a): both standard tautologies
terms.example: A true antecedent's parts already make the consequent true
terms.connects: Eliminated because they are always true

given.plan: Recognise A, C, D as standard valid laws, then hunt for a falsifying assignment of B by forcing the inner implication not b -> (a and c) to be false while a -> c is true.

steps.title: Eliminate the genuine tautologies
steps.formula_raw: A formula is a tautology iff it is true under every interpretation
steps.apply: A is ((a -> b) and (b -> c)) -> (a -> c), the hypothetical-syllogism law, always true. C is (a and b and c) -> (c or a): whenever the antecedent holds, a (and c) is true so the consequent c or a holds, always true. D is a -> (b -> a): if a is true the consequent b -> a is true, and if a is false the outer implication is vacuously true. So A, C, D are tautologies.
steps.note: Knowing the named laws lets you discard three options without full truth tables.

steps.title: Falsify option B with one assignment
steps.formula_raw: X -> Y is false exactly when X is true and Y is false
steps.apply: Take a=F, b=F, c=F. Then a -> c = F -> F = T (the antecedent of B). For the consequent: not b = T and a and c = F, so not b -> (a and c) = T -> F = F. Hence B = (a -> c) -> (not b -> (a and c)) = T -> F = F. One false row is enough, so B is NOT a tautology.
steps.note: Drive the inner implication to T -> F; that single counterexample settles it.

solution.result: Option B is false at a=F, b=F, c=F, so it is the expression that is NOT a tautology.

questions.formula_note: A tautology must be true on every row; the single assignment a=b=c=F makes B's outer implication T -> F = F, so B is not a tautology.

questions.question: Consider the following statements: P: Good mobile phones are not cheap. Q: Cheap mobile phones are not good. L: P implies Q. M: Q implies P. N: P is equivalent to Q. Which one of the following about L, M, and N is CORRECT? A. Only L is TRUE.  B. Only M is TRUE.  C. Only N is TRUE.  D. L, M and N are TRUE.
questions.answer: D. L, M and N are TRUE.
questions.to_find: Which of L (P -> Q), M (Q -> P), N (P <-> Q) is/are correct

understand.plain: Write the two sentences as conditionals. P says 'if a phone is good then it is not cheap', i.e. Good -> not Cheap. Q says 'if a phone is cheap then it is not good', i.e. Cheap -> not Good. The contrapositive of Good -> not Cheap is Cheap -> not Good, which is exactly Q. Since a statement and its contrapositive are logically equivalent, P and Q say the very same thing. Therefore P implies Q (L), Q implies P (M), and P is equivalent to Q (N) are all true, giving option D.

keywords.explain: 'All good phones are not cheap' translates to the implication Good -> not Cheap; the universal 'all' becomes 'if ... then'.
keywords.example: P: Good -> not Cheap; Q: Cheap -> not Good.

keywords.explain: The contrapositive of A -> B is not B -> not A, and it is logically equivalent to the original statement.
keywords.example: Contrapositive of Good -> not Cheap is Cheap -> not Good.

keywords.explain: If two statements have the same truth value in every situation, each implies the other and they are equivalent (P <-> Q).
keywords.example: P and Q being contrapositives makes L, M and N all true.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='208' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>P: Good -> not Cheap</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ink'>Q: Cheap -> not Good</text><text x='20' y='118' class='sub mut'>Q is the contrapositive of P</text><rect x='20' y='135' width='480' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='159' class='lbl ok'>P  ==  Q   =>   L, M, N all TRUE  (D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: P translated to Good -> not Cheap and Q to Cheap -> not Good are contrapositives, hence equivalent, so L, M and N are all true.

given.aim: Decide the truth of L: P -> Q, M: Q -> P and N: P <-> Q for the two given sentences.

terms.meaning: 'Good mobile phones are not cheap', i.e. Good -> not Cheap
terms.example: A good phone is guaranteed not cheap
terms.connects: Antecedent of L and consequent of M

terms.meaning: 'Cheap mobile phones are not good', i.e. Cheap -> not Good
terms.example: A cheap phone is guaranteed not good
terms.connects: The contrapositive of P; consequent of L and antecedent of M

terms.meaning: Cheap -> not Good is the contrapositive of Good -> not Cheap
terms.example: Negate and swap: not(not Cheap) -> not Good = Cheap -> not Good
terms.connects: Makes P and Q logically equivalent

terms.meaning: L = (P -> Q), M = (Q -> P), N = (P <-> Q): the three claims to evaluate
terms.example: All three hold once P == Q
terms.connects: Their joint truth selects the option

given.plan: Translate P and Q into conditionals, observe that Q is the contrapositive of P, conclude P and Q are equivalent, and read off that L, M and N are all true.

steps.title: Translate and take the contrapositive of P
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: P: 'Good phones are not cheap' is Good -> not Cheap. Its contrapositive is not(not Cheap) -> not Good = Cheap -> not Good, which is precisely statement Q. Since a statement is logically equivalent to its contrapositive, P and Q have the same truth value in every model, so P == Q.
steps.note: Negate both parts and swap them; double negation turns not(not Cheap) back into Cheap.

steps.title: Evaluate L, M and N
steps.formula_raw: P == Q  =>  (P -> Q), (Q -> P), (P <-> Q) all TRUE
steps.apply: Because P and Q are logically equivalent, P implies Q (so L is true), Q implies P (so M is true), and P is equivalent to Q (so N is true). All three statements hold, which is option D.
steps.note: Equivalence is exactly two-way implication plus matching truth values, so it forces L, M and N together.

solution.result: Q is the contrapositive of P, so P and Q are equivalent; hence L, M and N are all TRUE (option D).

questions.formula_note: Statement Q is the contrapositive of P, and a statement is equivalent to its contrapositive, so P == Q forces L, M and N all true, giving D.

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.latex:
eg(A \wedge B) \equiv (
eg A) \vee (
eg B), \quad 
eg(A \vee B) \equiv (
eg A) \wedge (
eg B)
formulas.plain: not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B); not(A or B) = (not A) and (not B)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to push a negation through a conjunction or disjunction, flipping AND to OR (and vice versa) while negating each operand; handy for decoding a 'false-in-one-row' truth table into a clean formula.
formulas.trap: The connective must flip: not(A and B) is (not A) or (not B), NOT (not A) and (not B). Forgetting to switch AND/OR is the usual error.
